content:
# Colour-Code Reference Chart

**Tool ID:** T010-01
**Block:** B010 (Cross-Contamination Prevention)
**Domain:** Food Safety & Hygiene (D01_food_safety)
**Level:** L1
**Format:** Guide
**Role:** Kitchen Lead
**Time Required:** 10-15 minutes to read; permanent display
**Version:** 1.0

---
## Welcome

This is a foundational tool -- the kind that prevents problems before they start. It is designed to be picked up and used immediately, no training required.

A practical guide that gives your team the knowledge they need, in language that makes sense on a busy cafe floor. Cross-contamination from raw to ready-to-eat food is one of the most common causes of foodborne illness. The colour-coded chopping board and utensil system is the industry-standard way to prevent it. This chart tells every team member which colour to use for what -- no guesswork.

**Built for Kitchen Lead working in Food Safety & Hygiene.**
Use it. Adapt it. Make it yours.

---

## Purpose

Laminated A4 chart showing the colour-coded chopping board and utensil system with the food type assigned to each colour, posted in the kitchen for quick reference. Ensures that raw meat never comes into contact with ready-to-eat food through shared surfaces.

This tool supports **Cross-Contamination Prevention** (B010) and is designed to be practical, printable, and immediately useful in a working cafe environment.

---

## How to Use This Tool

**Who:** Kitchen Lead (posts and enforces); All Kitchen Staff (follows daily)
**Time Required:** 10-15 minutes to read and learn; then permanent visual reference
**Frequency:** Referenced every time food preparation occurs

**What You'll Need:**
- This chart printed on A4, laminated, and posted at eye level in the preparation area
- A complete set of colour-coded chopping boards (minimum 4 colours)
- Colour-coded handled utensils (knives, tongs) if possible
- Storage rack that separates boards by colour

**Steps:**
1. Purchase colour-coded boards (sets available from catering suppliers, approx 20-40 GBP)
2. Print and laminate this chart
3. Post it directly above or beside the chopping board storage rack
4. Brief all kitchen staff during the next team meeting
5. Enforce the system from Day 1 -- no exceptions during busy service

---

## Guide

### The UK Standard Colour-Code System

| Colour | Food Type | Cafe Examples | Why Separate? |
|--------|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| **RED** | Raw meat and poultry | Slicing raw chicken for cooking, trimming bacon | Raw meat carries Salmonella, Campylobacter, E. coli. Must NEVER contact ready-to-eat food. |
| **BLUE** | Raw fish and seafood | Preparing tuna for sandwiches (if raw), handling smoked salmon | Raw fish carries different bacteria. Cross-contact with meat must also be avoided. |
| **YELLOW** | Cooked meat and poultry | Slicing cooked chicken for sandwiches, portioning cooked ham | Cooked meat is ready-to-eat. Must be kept separate from raw to prevent recontamination. |
| **GREEN** | Salad, fruit, and vegetables | Chopping tomatoes, slicing avocado, preparing fruit salad, garnishes | Eaten raw -- any contamination from raw meat on this board goes directly to the customer. |
| **WHITE** | Bakery, dairy, and bread | Slicing bread, cutting cheese, portioning butter, preparing sandwiches | General purpose for items not covered above. Often the most-used board in a cafe. |
| **BROWN** | Root vegetables and dirty vegetables | Peeling potatoes, trimming leeks, washing carrots | These items carry soil which can contain bacteria (e.g., Clostridium). Wash and peel on this board before transferring to green for slicing. |

### Cafe-Specific Application

In a typical cafe, these are the boards you will use most:

| Most Used | Typical Tasks |
|-----------|--------------|
| **GREEN** | All salad prep for sandwiches and wraps. Slicing tomatoes, cucumber, avocado. Preparing fruit for smoothies (Vitamix prep). Garnishes. |
| **WHITE** | All bread and sandwich assembly. Slicing sourdough, bagels, wraps. Cutting cakes and pastries for plating. Cheese boards. |
| **YELLOW** | Slicing pre-cooked chicken, ham, turkey for sandwich fillings. Portioning quiche. |
| **RED** | Less common in cafes but essential if you cook raw meat on site (e.g., cooking chicken for wraps). |

### Key Rules

1. **NEVER use a red board for ready-to-eat food** -- this is the most critical rule
2. **One board per food type** -- do not "quickly rinse" a board and reuse for a different category
3. **Wash boards in the dishwasher** between uses -- hot water (82C+) sanitises effectively. Hand washing alone is not sufficient for raw meat boards
4. **Replace boards when scored or damaged** -- deep cuts harbour bacteria that washing cannot remove. If you can catch a fingernail in a groove, the board needs replacing
5. **Store boards in a rack, separated by colour** -- do not stack them flat in a pile where raw meat residue can transfer
6. **Knives and tongs follow the same system** -- if possible, colour-code handles. At minimum, wash and sanitise knives between food types

### Common Mistakes

| Mistake | Risk | Fix |
|---------|------|-----|
| Using the nearest board regardless of colour | Raw meat bacteria transferred to salad items | Post this chart at eye level. Brief staff: colour matters every time. |
| Rinsing a board under the tap and reusing for a different food type | Rinsing does not remove bacteria -- only hot water (82C+) and detergent do | Board goes in the dishwasher or is sanitised with spray before reuse |
| Only owning 2 chopping boards | Forces shared use between food categories | Invest in a full set (4-6 boards). Budget approx 30 GBP. |
| Not replacing worn boards | Deep scores harbour bacteria invisible to the naked eye | Inspect boards monthly. Replace any with deep scoring. |
| Storing boards stacked flat | Moisture between boards + bacterial residue = growth environment | Use a board rack that stores them vertically with airflow |

### Quick Reference Table

| Situation | Recommended Action | Escalate If |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| You need to chop tomatoes for sandwiches | Use the GREEN board. If green board is in the dishwasher, wait for it or get a spare. Do NOT use red or blue. | No green boards available and service is starting -- inform Kitchen Lead immediately |
| You see a colleague using the red board for bread | Politely but immediately intervene: "That is the raw meat board -- let me grab you the white one." | If it happens repeatedly with the same person, inform the Manager for retraining |
| A chopping board is deeply scored | Take it out of service. Label it "REPLACE." Inform Kitchen Lead to order a replacement. | If multiple boards need replacing, escalate to Manager for budget approval |
| A board smells despite washing | The smell indicates bacterial contamination embedded in the surface. Discard the board. | Order a replacement before the next shift |

### Further Learning

- Related blocks: B009 (Handwashing Protocol), B011 (Cleaning Schedule Protocol)
- Ask the Kitchen Lead for a practical demonstration during prep time
- FSA guidance on preventing cross-contamination: "The 4 Cs -- Cross-contamination"

---

## Common Risks

### Risk 1: Colour System Abandoned During Busy Service

**Why it happens:** During the lunch rush, the Kitchen Lead grabs the nearest board because the correct one is in the dishwasher. "Just this once" becomes the standard.
**How to prevent:** Own enough boards for peak service. At minimum: 2 green, 2 white, 1 yellow, 1 red. That way, one can be in use while the other is being washed. A spare board costs 5-8 GBP -- a food poisoning incident costs the business.
**If it is already happening:** Buy spare boards this week. Brief the team: "We now have enough boards for every task. There is no reason to use the wrong colour."

### Risk 2: New Staff Not Trained on the System

**Why it happens:** A new team member starts on a Saturday. It is busy, nobody explains the colour system, and they use whichever board is closest. By the time someone notices, they have been doing it wrong for a week.
**How to prevent:** Colour-code training is a Day 1 induction item. It takes 5 minutes. Walk the new starter through this chart, show them the board rack, and ask them to tell you which colour they would use for three common tasks.
**If it is already happening:** Brief them today. Do not wait for a quiet moment -- cross-contamination risk is immediate.

### Risk 3: Boards Not Replaced When Worn

**Why it happens:** Boards get scored and stained over time. Staff get used to how they look and stop noticing the damage. But deep grooves in a chopping board harbour bacteria that no amount of washing can remove.
**How to prevent:** Monthly board inspection by the Kitchen Lead. Run a fingernail across the surface -- if you feel grooves, the board needs replacing. Budget for 2-3 replacement boards per year as a normal operating cost.
**If it is already happening:** Inspect all boards today. Remove any that are deeply scored, warped, or discoloured. Replace this week.

---

## Customisation Notes

**This tool is a starting point, not a rulebook.**

**Independent / Specialty Cafe:** You may not handle raw meat on site. If so, you can skip the red board -- but keep it if you ever introduce any raw meat cooking. Your most-used boards will be green (salad) and white (bread/sandwiches).
**Chain / Franchise:** Your brand may use a specific colour-code system. Check for consistency. This chart follows the UK industry standard.
**Kiosk / Mobile:** Space is limited. At minimum, you need green (salad/fruit) and white (bread/general). Store them separately even in a small space.
**Bakery Cafe:** Add emphasis on the white board for all bakery items. Consider a dedicated board for allergen-free baking if you offer gluten-free products.

**Making it yours:**
- Highlight the 2-3 boards your team uses most
- Add your specific menu prep tasks next to each colour
- Post this chart where it is visible during prep, not hidden behind a door
- Include board inspection in your monthly kitchen audit

The structure matters more than the specific fields. Keep the structure, change the content.

---

## Connected Resources

**Other tools in this block:**
- T010-02 -- Cross-Contamination Prevention SOP (L2, SOP)
- T010-03 -- Weekly Kitchen Cross-Contamination Inspection (L2, checklist)
- T010-04 -- Kitchen Workflow Risk Map (L2, template)
- T010-05 -- Quarterly Cross-Contamination Audit (L3, audit)

**Related blocks:**
- B009
- B011

**Parent block:** B010 -- Cross-Contamination Prevention
**Module:** M01: Food Safety & Hygiene Compliance
---

*Tool Version 1.0 -- OIS Cafe v2*
*Built for cafe operators who value clarity, consistency, and craft.*
